fields.Name: Four-seam fastball
fields.Movement: “Rising” / straight, stays up
fields.Deception: Pure velocity, gets on hitter fast

fields.Name: Sinker / two-seam
fields.Movement: Arm-side run + sink
fields.Deception:
Velocity plus ground-ball movement
"Ground-ball movement" refers to the kind of pitch movement that tends to produce ground balls when the hitter makes contact — and a sinker is the classic example.
The mechanism is about where the bat meets the ball. A sinker has arm-side run and sink — it drops more than a four-seam fastball over the last several feet. The hitter, expecting the ball to stay on a flatter plane, swings on a slightly higher path than where the ball actually ends up. The bat catches the top half of the ball rather than squaring it up. Hitting the top half drives the ball downward into the dirt — a ground ball. Contrast a four-seamer, which "stays up" relative to expectation and tends to get hit in the air (fly balls, line drives) or fouled straight back.
So "ground-ball movement" is shorthand for: late downward action that makes hitters mishit the ball on top. Pitchers and analysts care about this because ground balls are low-risk outcomes — a grounder almost never becomes an extra-base hit and frequently becomes a double play, whereas balls in the air carry home-run and gap-double risk. This is why a pitcher's ground-ball rate (GB%) is a tracked stat, and why "sinker/groundball pitcher" is a recognized archetype — Brandon Webb and Dallas Keuchel are textbook cases, both of whom built careers on inducing weak contact into the ground rather than missing bats.
It connects to the spin discussion from earlier, too. A sinker's sink comes partly from a spin axis tilted away from pure backspin, so it generates less of the lift that holds a four-seamer up. Less backspin lift means more drop, more drop means more top-half contact, and that means grounders.

fields.Name: Cutter
fields.Movement: Small, late glove-side movement
fields.Deception: Looks like a fastball, breaks off the barrel

fields.Name: Slider
fields.Movement: Glove-side sweep, some drop

fields.Name: Curveball
fields.Movement: Big downward (12 to 6 ) break

fields.Name: Changeup
fields.Movement: Arm-side fade + drop
fields.Deception: Fastball look, velocity drop fools timing

fields.Name: Splitter
fields.Movement: Sharp late drop
fields.Deception: Fastball look, dives from low spin

fields.Name: Forkball
fields.Movement: Bigger, slower tumbling drop. Like a splitter, but with a less dramatic, more gradual downward movement.

fields.Name: Knuckleball
fields.Spin: Near-zero — the goal is no spin
fields.Movement: Erratic, unpredictable flutter — different every pitch
fields.Deception: Chaotic movement no one can predict

fields.Name: Slurve
fields.Movement: Glove-side sweep plus downward drop — between a slider and a curve
fields.Deception: Spin-driven break combining a slider's sweep with a curve's drop

fields.Name: Screwball
fields.Spin: High; sidespin that runs arm-side, often with some topspin
fields.Movement: Arm-side break plus dramatic drop
fields.Deception: Spin-driven break that moves arm-side, the direction hitters rarely see
